title: The Hidden Costs of No Action Dispositions and the High Savings of Preauthorizing Arrests with Prosecutors
abstract:
With one procedural change, jurisdictions can save millions of dollars, keep thousands of innocents out of jail, and strengthen their cases against guilty offenders. 
In most jurisdictions, a police officer arrests and books a defendant, and then files the criminal complaint before a judge. The judge sets bail, and if necessary, appoints defense counsel for the accused. In theory, the judge rejects those arrests that lack sufficient detail or fail to meet the legal elements of the purported crime, and only the complaints that overcome this check are passed to the local prosecutors. In practice, however, it is often administrative clerks who accept arrests. In many jurisdictions, these clerks lack legal training and are ill equipped to evaluate the merits of each case, resulting in deference to the police officer’s on-site decision. Inadequate cases are later dropped by the prosecutors with the disposition of No Action—but only after extensive investigation and usually not before the accused spent one or more nights in detention. In 1973, Harris County, Texas introduced a new method of intake for accused defendants: prosecutors receive a phone call from the police officer at the moment of arrest. The officer explains the circumstances of the arrest, and the prosecutor evaluates whether it meets the required elements of the crime. If satisfied, the prosecutor grants authorization, and only then does the police officer file. 
We quantify the efficacy of a pre-authorization intake system by comparing arrest records filed in Harris County, TX, Miami-Dade County, FL, and dozens of counties in New Mexico. We find that a pre-authorization intake system costs on average $1.8-2.9 million less per 100,000 residents than the traditional method of arrests that occur before expert legal evaluation of the strengths or weaknesses of the underlying facts of the case.
